text.title: Job Description Writing and Candidate Personas
text.summary: Frameworks for writing JDs that attract qualified candidates and developing candidate personas that define the target profile before sourcing begins.

rule.statement: Limit must-have requirements to 5-7 items and nice-to-have to 3-4; JDs listing 15+ requirements reduce qualified applicant volume without improving hire quality.
rule.rationale: Research (LinkedIn, Textio) shows that long requirement lists disproportionately deter underrepresented candidates who self-screen out if they don't meet every requirement. Reducing to true requirements increases qualified applicant volume.

section.title: JD Structure
section.p: Hook: why this role matters to the company and the market (2-3 sentences). Impact: what the hire will accomplish in the first year (concrete outcomes, not job duties). Requirements: 5-7 must-have skills or experiences, each grounded in actual job tasks. Nice-to-have: 3-4 bonus qualifications that differentiate candidates. Benefits: compensation range (required in many jurisdictions), equity, perks. Culture: team size, working style, decision-making norms.

antipattern.description: Publishing a JD copied from a prior job posting without updating requirements to reflect the current role.
antipattern.reason: Stale JDs attract candidates for the previous role, not the current one. Misalignment surfaces in screening and wastes recruiter and candidate time.

rule.statement: Define the target candidate persona before writing the JD or opening sourcing channels; without a profile, sourcing generates noise and screening becomes subjective.
rule.rationale: A persona converts the hiring manager's vague "someone like our best performer" into specific, sourceable attributes: experience range, current role, motivations, channels. This makes sourcing targeted and screening criteria explicit.

section.title: Persona Components
section.p: Demographics: experience range (years), current title or adjacent titles, industries with transferable background.
Motivations: what career goal does this role advance? What values must the company demonstrate? What are their deal-breakers (e.g., no remote, no equity)?
Channels: where do they spend time online? How do they search for roles (referrals, LinkedIn, community)? What content do they consume?
Messaging: what value proposition resonates (growth, mission, compensation, flexibility)? What objections must the JD or outreach preempt?

example.note: Persona: "Senior Software Engineer, 5-8 years, current role at a Series B-C startup or scaleup. Motivated by technical autonomy and ownership. Searches via LinkedIn and engineering community newsletters. Objection: will we have greenfield work or inherit legacy code? Messaging: lead on new infra stack, no approval layers for architectural decisions."
